summary: I was born and have spent the majority of my life in Owensboro, Kentucky, since 1973. After fifteen years away, including my time in the Navy and at Western Kentucky University, I returned to Owensboro to work in education. I currently serve as a college and career coach at Apollo High School, my alma mater, and work with local alternative education programs here. Owensboro has been the center of my personal and professional life.

I spent fifteen years serving in the United States Navy, which was a significant part of my early adult life. This experience took me away from Owensboro for a long period and shaped my discipline and perspective. After my Navy service, I transitioned to education and higher learning at Western Kentucky University. The Navy was foundational before I settled into my career in education.

After my time in the Navy, I attended Western Kentucky University to further my education. This was a stepping stone that helped me move into teaching and educational administration roles. My studies there prepared me for my first teaching job and later leadership positions in schools. WKU played a key role in my career development after military service.

Apollo High School is my alma mater where I graduated in 1992, and later returned to work as a college and career coach. I helped create the College and Career Coach position there through a federal grant. Working at Apollo has been meaningful because it connects my past as a student with my present role supporting students' futures. It’s a place where I’ve invested a lot of effort to improve opportunities for young people.

Race to the Top was a federal grant program that enabled me to create the College and Career Coach position at Apollo High School. This grant supported about thirty coaches across Kentucky, from Northern Kentucky to Bowling Green and Hodgenville. It was instrumental in establishing a new role focused on helping students transition from school to careers or college. The grant helped launch a statewide initiative that I helped lead.
cues: created College and Career Coach position

summary: The Green River Regional Educational Cooperative employed me as a College and Career Coach, even though I worked physically at Apollo High School. This cooperative covered a broad area in Kentucky and was part of the Race to the Top initiative. It provided the framework and support for my role in helping students with college and career readiness. Being part of this cooperative connected me to a larger network of educators across the region.
cues: part of Race to the Top
support for college and career readiness

summary: Kentucky has been the backdrop for my entire life and career, from growing up in Owensboro to working in various educational roles across the state. I've been involved in statewide initiatives like the College and Career Coaches Association and legislative work to improve alternative education. Serving on the Kentucky Association of School Administrators board and receiving state awards also reflect my deep connection to Kentucky's educational community. The state's challenges and opportunities have shaped much of my work.

I have served on the board of the Kentucky Association of School Administrators for the past three years. This role has allowed me to engage in education policy work and advocate for school leaders across the state. My involvement with KASA helped lead to recognition like the David Karem Educational Policy Excellence Award. Being part of this organization connects me to the broader leadership community in Kentucky education.

entity: David Karem Educational Policy Excellence Award
summary: I am honored to be the recipient of the David Karem Educational Policy Excellence Award this year, though the announcement is still under wraps. This award recognizes my legislative efforts to expand access to the graduate equivalency diploma for alternative education students in Kentucky. It’s a gratifying acknowledgment of the work I’ve done to improve educational opportunities and policies. Receiving this award highlights the impact of my advocacy and leadership.

In September of this year, I was inducted into the Kentucky Veterans Hall of Fame, which was a humbling experience. This honor reflects my fifteen years of service in the United States Navy and my ongoing commitment to the community. Being recognized among other veterans in Kentucky is meaningful and adds to the sense of pride I have for my military background. It’s been a recent highlight alongside my educational achievements.

The COVID-19 pandemic was a time of rapid change and adaptability for me professionally. When schools closed in March 2020, we had to quickly shift to distance learning without knowing how long it would last or what resources students had at home. It was especially challenging for students in alternative education who faced high trauma and poverty. Throughout the pandemic, we continuously adapted to new guidelines while trying to keep students connected and supported despite the chaos.

entity: Kentucky Educational Cooperative for State Agency Children
summary: The Kentucky Educational Cooperative for State Agency Children oversees programs for students in mental health hospitals, youth detention centers, and other state placements. Many of these students come from challenging backgrounds, including those in foster care or group homes like the Hayden Home in Owensboro. This cooperative manages education for kids who often cannot attend traditional schools due to their circumstances. My work involves understanding and supporting these diverse and vulnerable populations.

The Department of Juvenile Justice is involved in the education of youth placed in detention or long-term facilities due to criminal acts. Many of the students I work with in alternative education come through DJJ group homes or youth detention centers. We collaborate with DJJ staff to provide educational services and support behavioral and drug violation cases. This partnership is essential for helping these youth stay on track academically despite their challenging situations.

The Department for Community Based Services plays a role in the lives of many students I serve, especially those in foster care or custody situations. Some students placed through DCBS live in facilities like the Hayden Home in Owensboro, which provides care for kids who cannot remain with their families. DCBS involvement often means these students face additional challenges that impact their education. Understanding their circumstances helps tailor the alternative education programs to meet their needs.

Hayden Home in Owensboro is a facility for children who cannot live with their families and are placed there for their best interest. We currently have students from Hayden Home enrolled in our alternative education programs. This place represents a critical support system for kids facing difficult family situations. Working with students from Hayden Home requires sensitivity to their backgrounds and extra care to help them succeed academically.

Daviess County High School is one of the large traditional high schools in Owensboro with around eighteen hundred to nineteen hundred students. Compared to alternative education settings, it has a much larger and more chaotic environment. Some students who struggle in such large schools may find alternative education programs more suitable due to smaller class sizes and more structure. Daviess County High represents the traditional school environment many of our alternative students transition from or to.

Heritage Park High School is one of the district-run alternative education programs in Owensboro, where class sizes are much smaller, usually twelve to fifteen students. I have taught and administered in such alternative settings, which provide more structure and individualized attention than larger schools. Heritage Park serves students who need a different environment due to behavioral, mental health, or academic challenges. It’s a critical part of the alternative education landscape in our community.

ACT WorkKeys is a test used by local factories and employers to assess basic career and academic skills like reading and math. Many of the students in alternative education programs need to meet these minimum standards to be considered hirable. Part of my role is to help prepare students to pass WorkKeys so they can enter the workforce successfully. It’s a practical benchmark that guides much of the career readiness work we do.

The General Educational Development, or GED, is now available again as an option for all alternative education students in Kentucky, thanks to recent legislation. Previously, only state agency children qualified for the GED, but now about twenty-three thousand kids in alternative programs can access it. This provides a vital last chance for students who are credit deficient to earn a credential and move on to work or further education. Expanding GED access has been a major focus of my policy work.

While I didn’t discuss fentanyl directly in detail, drug violations are a significant issue among the students I work with in alternative education. My role has involved overseeing units that deal with behavioral and drug violations at the high school level. The presence of substances like fentanyl in the community adds to the challenges these students face. Addressing drug-related issues is part of the broader effort to support at-risk youth in our programs.
